data.title: Housing Starts Jump 7.2% In January, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Housing starts jumped 7.2% in January to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.487 million, exceeding the forecast of 1.340 million. This is the highest level in eleven months and marks the third consecutive monthly increase. Housing starts are up 9.5% compared to one year ago.

Background on Housing Starts

Housing starts track how many residential buildings began construction in the preceding month. The data is divided into three types of structures: single-family homes, residences with 2-4 units (condos or townhouses), and structures with 5+ units (apartment complexes). A critical aspect of the home-building industry is the powerful influence it has on the rest of the economy.

Here is the historical series for total privately owned housing starts, which dates from 1959. Because of the extreme volatility of the monthly data points, a six-month moving average has been included.

Housing Starts: Structure Breakdown

In January, single-family housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.935 million. This represents a 2.8% decline from December and a 6.5% drop from one year ago.

Multi-family building housing starts were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 0.524 million, their highest level since May 2023. This represents a 29.1% increase from December and a 56.9% rise from one year ago.

Note: Buildings with 2-4 units only have N.S.A. data available because tests for identifiable and stable seasonality do not meet reliability standards.

Housing Starts: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Here is the data with a simple population adjustment. The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show substantial growth in the US population since 1959. Here is a chart of housing starts as a percent of the population. We've added a linear regression through the monthly data to highlight the trend.

Housing Starts: A Footnote on Volatility

The extreme volatility of this monthly indicator is the rationale for paying more attention to its six-month moving average than to its noisy monthly change. Over the complete data series, the absolute month-over-month average percent change was 6.3%. The month-over-month range minimum was -26.4% and the maximum was 29.3%.

For visual confirmation of the volatility, here is a snapshot of the monthly percent change since 1960.

A Long-Term Look: Residential Building Permits and Housing Starts

In addition to housing starts, the U.S. Census Bureau and the Department of Housing and Urban Development published their findings for new residential building permits. Building permits typically preceded housing starts, offering a glimpse into future construction activity.

Despite the fact that both are monthly seasonally adjusted annualized rate (SAAR) series, they are exceptionally volatile and subject to extensive revisions. Thus it is unwise to assign much significance to a single month.

Over the long haul, however, the two offer a compelling study of trends in residential real estate, especially when we adjust the permits and starts for population growth. Here is an overlay of the two series since the 1959 inception of the starts data and the 1960 inception of the permits data.

The monthly data points are preserved as faint dots. The trends are illustrated with six-month moving averages of data divided by the Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates.

Here is a closer look at the overlay since 1990.

ETFs associated with home builders include Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

ETFs associated with residential real estate include iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

data.title: Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund FY 2025: What Worked... And What Didn't
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund FY 2025 Commentary.

Nine of the portfolio’s 10 equity sectors made a positive impact on calendar year performance, with Health Care, Industrials, and Materials making the largest positive contributions while the only negative impact came from Consumer Staples. At the industry level, health care providers & services (Health Care), aerospace & defense (Industrials), and pharmaceuticals (Health Care) contributed most, while software (Information Technology), commercial services & supplies (Industrials), and food products (Consumer Staples) were the largest detractors.

Our top-contributing position was Palvella Therapeutics (PTX), a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused on developing and commercializing novel therapies to treat patients suffering from serious, rare genetic skin diseases for which there are no FDA-approved therapies. Palvella is developing a broad pipeline of product candidates based on its patented QTORIN platform—a proprietary technology that focuses on delivering highly targeted technologies directly to the disease source. Palvella’s shares appreciated significantly in 2025 as investors grew more confident in the company’s pipeline following a grant from the FDA, patent wins, expanded indications and programs, and a positive Phase II readout, with an additional Phase III readout expected in 1Q26. QTORIN rapamycin has the potential to be the only approved therapy in multiple rare disease settings, supporting a potential $2 billion+ commercial opportunity. Palvella has recently expanded its pipeline to address additional opportunities across rare skin diseases. We began building a position in February of 2025, when it was a mostly undiscovered, and uncovered, company, with just a few sell side analysts covering the stock; today, there are 15.

Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) operates a telehealth consultation platform that provides consumers access to healthcare professionals for mental health, sexual health, dermatology, and primary care. Its shares outperformed in 2025 as the company saw positive revisions from +37% to +59% revenue growth expectations for 2025 that were driven by the company’s position in the weight loss segment. The company manufacturers a generic version of the GLP-1 via compounding operations that led its stock to rally on news that it had signed a distribution partnership agreement with leading branded manufacturer, Novo Nordisk (NVO). Shortly afterward, however, Novo Nordisk announced it had terminated the partnership. We began trimming the position on those headlines, ultimately exiting the position as our analysis showed a deteriorating risk-reward.

Impinj (PI) is a leading RAIN RFID provider helping businesses analyze, optimize, and innovate by wirelessly connecting everyday items to their systems. In short, Impinj’s tags can be attached to anything, from shipping boxes to items on the shelves in retail stores, to fresh food items in the grocery store. The tags are “read” from scanners and logged in real time on their systems, allowing customers to track data points relevant to each respective business and thus properly track and manage inventory at an item level. Impinj is a long time holding in the portfolio with a very large TAM (total addressable market) expansion opportunity, a strong patent portfolio, and an increasingly diversified customer base. After trimming our position in 2024 as their retail channel customers went through a period of digestion, we began adding to the position again early in 2025 once that period of digestion was complete, and we began to see positive revisions to earnings estimates.

AeroVironment (AVAV) designs, develops, produces, and supports the operation of unmanned aircraft systems and electric transportation solutions. The stock recovered after a sell-off on softer quarterly earnings earlier in 2025, followed by exceptional fiscal fourth quarter results (reported in April) and a spotlight on unmanned aerial vehicles becoming a larger part of the Department of Defense budget, as the U.S. looks to modernize its equipment and resources. Defense technology continues to be an area of investor focus entering 2026 and beyond given the potential of significant increases in military spending due to geopolitical uncertainty.

JFrog (FROG) helps enterprises and their software developers track, secure, and protect “binaries,” or open source code developed outside the enterprise’s servers or systems of record. The company should benefit from the expected growth of AI-generated code. JFrog reported better-than-expected earnings and revenue in November, driven by success in its Cloud and Security segments and also raised its forecast for earnings and revenue for fiscal 2025 as a whole.

The biggest detractor at the holdings level was ACV Auctions (ACVA), which provides used car wholesale auction services. Its stock underperformed in 2025 due to a deceleration of organic growth (after a robust 3Q24 of +30%), increased competition, and a continued weak used car auction environment. It remains a top holding in the Fund, given the potential for an industry rebound and several new growth drivers over the next three years.

Freshpet (FRPT), the leading “fresh” ingredient and unfrozen pet food maker, endured decelerating organic growth after outperforming in 2023 and 2024 from improved capital management and production efficiencies. The Consumer Discretionary sector struggled in 2025, and despite growing faster than most businesses in the sector, Freshpet experienced valuation contraction along with the slower growth and estimate cuts. We have used the pullback as an opportunity to add to what we think is a high quality growth company.

An innovator in the restaurant industry payment solutions market, PAR Technology (PAR) has continued to win new business from new and existing large restaurant chain customers but has also seen delays in project implementation from many customers. Its backlog continues to build, and we have used the pullback to add to our position, expecting revenue to accelerate from what should be a temporary slowdown.

Semtech (SMTC) is a semiconductor company that has been a successful turnaround after making a poor acquisition several years ago. New management have cut costs, increased exposure to data center infrastructure applications, and expressed interest in selling what is left of the acquired company (Sierra Wireless). We believe context is important with regard to this position as it was a massive outperformer in 2024, when its share price nearly quadrupled, before management announced a slower ramp in their datacenter product growth rate in early 2025, causing the shares to drop precipitously. Our timing of reducing and then adding back to this position was detrimental to performance, despite the stock itself finishing up over the calendar 12 month period.

Alvotech (ALVO) is a biopharmaceutical company that develops, manufactures, and distributes biosimilar medicines, which are lower-cost versions of complex biologic drugs whose patents have expired. These biosimilars treat serious diseases such as cancer, autoimmune disorders, and other conditions. In early November, Alvotech received a “complete response letter” from the FDA regarding its Biologics License Application for AVT05, a key product, in which the agency cited deficiencies following an inspection of its Reykjavik manufacturing site. The company then lowered its 2025 revenue and earnings guidance, which prompted us to exit our position since the investment case was based on a fourth-quarter launch of new products.

The Fund’s advantage over its benchmark came solely from stock selection in 2025 (sector allocation was marginally negative). At the sector level, stock selection in Health Care gave the Fund an outsized relative advantage, followed by stock selection in Materials and Energy. Conversely, stock selection and, to a lesser extent, a lower weighting detracted in Industrials. Stock selection also detracted in Consumer Staples (where our lower exposure was a positive), as did a combination of stock selection and a higher weighting in Information Technology.

data.title: Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund FY 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Performance

In a good year for small-cap growth stocks, Royce Smaller-Companies Growth Fund (RYVPX) made an excellent showing on both an absolute and relative basis. The Fund advanced 19.3% in 2025, beating both Russell 2000 Growth Index, which was up 13.0%, and the Russell 2000 Index, which gained 12.8%, for the same period. The Fund also outperformed both indexes for the 3-year, 10-year, and since inception (6/14/01) periods ended 12/31/25 while also outpacing the small-cap growth index for the 5-year period.

Hims & Hers Health Cl. A
Impinj
AeroVironment
JFrog
1 Includes dividends

Top Detractors from PerformanceFor 20252

ACV Auctions Cl. A
Freshpet
PAR Technology
Semtech Corporation
Alvotech
2 Net of dividends

Current Positioning and Outlook

Despite much noise and recent volatility, we are enthusiastic about the potential of small-caps to power ahead over the next several years. The Trump administration’s policies should continue to drive the economy and markets; it is increasingly clear that fiscal spending will continue (at the risk of growing the federal budget deficits) to rebuild aging defense and other infrastructure while developing domestic supplies of uranium, lithium, and other rare-earth materials. With a new Federal Reserve Chair looking more likely in 2026, a more accommodative money supply and rate environment may also contribute to economic growth across businesses, government, and consumers. AI, automation, and innovation of all sorts appear to be durable themes, despite the over-investment and supply chain disruptions that often occur during the early years of a major innovation. At the same time, we witnessed several pockets of speculative activity in 2025 that need to be monitored, particularly where valuations raced ahead of fundamentals, and in some cases lacked any fundamentals at all.

Our positioning continues to reflect our focus on growing companies in growth industries while managing positions (or themes) that have become overvalued in the short term. There are risks in every business and industry, of course, and we attempt to balance the risk/reward equation based on hard data, whether economic, company-specific, or valuation-related. New themes will emerge, while others will mature, a key component of our daily research efforts. It was also noteworthy that some of the highest quality small-cap growth companies underperformed during the year, offering better relative valuations than we have seen in some time, another development that bolsters our confidence in the small cap universe.

data.title: Lazard Global Small Cap Equity Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Global small cap equities rose in the fourth quarter, with signs that market leadership was beginning to broaden. Notably, the months-long AI-driven rally appeared to enter a new phase, with investors becoming increasingly cautious about the sustainability of elevated AI capex. Many perceived AI-winners (mainly companies investing in or enabling AI infrastructure) sold off as the pace of spending on chips and data centers, which has been significantly reliant on debt financing, showed no signs of moderating, leading many to question the returns such investments would ultimately generate. However, AI-linked stocks staged a recovery in the closing weeks of the period.

In the United States, investors cheered as the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates at three consecutive policy meetings despite disagreements among officials about the path ahead. At its December policy meeting, the central bank hinted that it would likely pause further cuts as it collects more data to assess labor market and inflation conditions. Investors also reacted positively to a delayed release of third-quarter US GDP data showing stronger than expected growth driven by resilient consumer spending, powering stocks in the region to new highs toward the end of the period.

Meanwhile in Europe, stocks advanced after the European Central Bank held interest rates steady amid signs that the eurozone's economic outlook was improving, suggesting its easing cycle was largely complete. The Bank of England lowered interest rates in December to their lowest level in nearly three years but signaled a slower pace of future reductions. Central banks in Switzerland, Sweden, and Norway left their key interest rates unchanged during the quarter.

In Japan, equities rose but lagged the broader global index as investor enthusiasm for the elevation of a new, business-friendly prime minister was tempered by the Bank of Japan's renewed rate-hiking cycle. The central bank raised its main interest rate to a 30-year high and signaled a willingness to tighten further as it confronts elevated inflation. Elsewhere in Asia, stocks in China declined amid weak economic data and the central bank's decision to hold lending rates steady despite the country's economic woes.

Portfolio Review

During the fourth quarter, the Lazard Global Small Cap Equity Portfolio outperformed its blended benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index through 31 August 2025, and MSCI World Small Cap Index thereafter (net of fees), by returning 3.0% compared to the Index's 2.8%.

Portfolio Drivers

What Helped

  • Shares of US-listed optical laser producer Lumentum (LITE) (2.4% weighting in the Portfolio) rose after the company reported solid quarterly results, driven by strong margin performance and new customer wins.
  • Coherent (COHR) (2.1% weighting in the Portfolio), a US-listed global leader in lasers and semiconductors, particularly optical transceivers (small components that reliably connect switches and servers in a data center), rose along with peers amid encouraging AI capex datapoints.

What Hurt

  • Shares of Swiss electrical infrastructure product maker R&S Group (0.0% weighting in the Portfolio) fell after management issued a downward guidance revision, citing reduced installation capacity at certain customers. We sold our position during the period.
  • Shares of US-listed mining company Ramaco (METC) (1.1% weighting in the Portfolio) fell following strong outperformance year-to-date, which had been fueled by investor optimism around the company's plans to produce rare earths.

data.title: Positive Sentiment Streak At An End
data.description:
This week's sentiment gauges saw some big moves. As we noted in last night's Closer, the Charles Schwab (SCHW) Schwab Trading Activity Index, or STAX for short, experienced a near-record increase in February.

Meanwhile, other weekly sentiment gauges have deteriorated. The AAII survey is a prime example, as bullish sentiment fell to 31.9% this week. That is the sixth consecutive weekly decline, bringing bullish sentiment to the lowest level since the week of 11/13.

That drop in bulls was met with a double-digit percentage point surge in bears. Bearish sentiment leaped from 35.5% last week, a one-month low, to 46.4% today. That is the highest reading for bears since the week of 10/16 and the largest one-week increase since November.

Put together, the bull-bear spread has now been negative (meaning there are more bears than bulls) for the fourth week in a row. The 12.1 point drop this week was the biggest WoW decline since the week of 11/13, and the spread is also the lowest since that same week.

In other words, the AAII survey has taken a decisively negative tone partway through the second week of conflict with Iran. Likewise, the same can be said for other surveys, such as the NAAIM Exposure Index and the Investors Intelligence survey.

The former showed active managers reporting as the least aggressively long since the final week of last April during the tariff tantrum. Meanwhile, bulls dropped below 50% in the Investors Intelligence survey for the first time since November. Putting it all together, our Sentiment Composite is back to negative territory following over six months of positive readings.

As shown below, this week snapped a streak of 29 consecutive weeks of positive readings in the sentiment composite. With the streak over, it ends as the fifth-longest streak on record.

data.title: Hormuz Crisis Is Forcing Europe And Japan Into Hawkish Mode: Is The U.S. Next?
data.description:
As I said in my last coverage, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz looks quite distant.

Since then, the recent developments have only reinforced my view.

Attacks on tankers and energy infrastructure across the Gulf have continued to pile up, raising the number to 16 (with 6 of them on March 11), while oil markets are once again pricing in the risk of a prolonged supply shock.

At the time of writing, Brent is back in the mid-$90s after dropping to $80 on Tuesday. I find it quite impressive that this increase occurred despite the IEA release of 400 million barrels from emergency reserves, with the United States contributing 172 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve.

The fear of inflation is high on both sides of the Atlantic and the Pacific. In Europe, policymakers are beginning to acknowledge that a sustained rise in oil prices could once again complicate the inflation outlook and delay any confidence around easier policy. Bear in mind the ECB hasn't cut since June 2025.

In Japan, the concern may be even more severe given the country’s heavy dependence on Middle Eastern energy imports (95% of imported oil) and its direct exposure to disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz. With 70% of Japan’s imported oil passing through this strait, I think Japan is the prime example right now for how an external energy shock can feed into domestic prices, raise inflation expectations, and push central banks to maintain a hawkish stance for longer.

I would keep a very close eye on any policy news coming from these two regions. Below, I explain why.

Europe And Japan Turning Hawkish?

As I'm typing these words, Brent oil is trading in the mid $90s/bbl as the overall situation in the Middle East appears to be moving toward escalation rather than easing.

Iranian attacks are now spreading across the energy and port infrastructure across the Gulf. One notable incident involved two fuel tankers in Iraqi territorial waters that were struck by Iranian boats. As a response, Iraq halted operations at its oil terminals.

This raises the total number of ships struck in the region to at least 16. Bear in mind that 6 of those attacks happened on March 11 alone, including a recent attack on a container ship.

That’s why I was highly skeptical in my previous coverage about a near-term reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Interestingly enough, some sources indicate that Iran’s crude oil exports averaged between 1.1 million barrels per day and 1.5 million bpd from February 28 through March 11. For context, the country’s average exports last year were 1.69 million bpd. So, some oil is getting out, but it's still far from the 20 million barrels per day of crude oil and petroleum liquids that transit the strait on average.

You may remember some of Trump's remarks earlier this week that tanked oil prices:

I think the war is very complete, pretty much.

We're very far ahead of schedule.

Wrapping up is all in my mind, nobody else's.

We have sanctions on some countries. We're going to take those sanctions off till the Strait is up.

In fact, in a recent interview, Trump said, "Any time I want it to end, it will end."

However, later during the same day, I noticed a different tone at his Kentucky rally: "We won," but "we don’t want to leave early," and "we've got to finish the job."

Wasn't the war "very complete"?

Well, reading Trump's comments and trading on that often leads to no good based on my past experience.

In my view, similar to what happened in the Bab el-Mandeb Strait after the Houthis' attacks in 2023/24, once the hostilities come to an end, the shipping industry prefers to take no risks (mainly due to the war risk insurance premium). Why?

Because even after the Gaza ceasefire was agreed upon in October 2025, there are still incidents like the one below on February 18, 2026:

Given that some sources suggest that Iran had laid about a dozen mines in the Strait of Hormuz, I don't think any major operator will take the risk to traverse the strait as long as the conflict is ongoing, even if military vessels (eventually) decide to escort them.

Bottom line, oil prices are up, and Europe and Japan are now preparing to respond faster than in the 2021-22 shock.

On Europe's side, Bundesbank President Joachim Nagel told Reuters on March 11 that the ECB would act decisively if the current oil shock began translating into broad inflation over the medium term.

To be clear, the impact is already showing up at gas stations, with some analysts projecting €220 extra per year in fuel spending for an average European driver if oil stays near $100 per barrel.

So far, no policymaker has called for a hike at the March 19 meeting. However, the following remarks from Peter Kazimir, ​one of the most outspoken policy hawks, raised my eyebrows:

I’d say a reaction by ​the ECB is potentially closer than many people think.

Overall, I would keep a close eye on any comments that suggest a near-term "reaction" at the March 19 meeting. As seen below, the ECB stopped cutting rates in June 2025, and a rate hike would be negative for the markets.

Moving on to Japan, back on March 3, the BOJ was inclined to hold hikes for the March 18-19 meeting because policymakers wanted more time to assess the economic damage from higher oil prices and the weaker yen.

Well, given the fact that Japan is a major energy importer, a rise in crude feeds directly into domestic costs. The problem that I see is that Japan imports close to 95% of its crude oil from the Middle East. In fact, some sources suggest that 70% of Japan’s oil imports pass through the Strait of Hormuz.

Therefore, I think Japan is the best candidate to monitor from a rate policy perspective, given its high exposure to the Hormuz disruption.

For reference, the BOJ was already hawkish since March 2024 after raising interest rates due to sustained inflation in the country. I think the hike in oil prices could be a good reason for another hike as soon as April this year.

The risk here is that these hawkish comments and policies could spread globally, eventually affecting the United States. I discuss this in the next section.

What's Next

This week, the IEA members agreed on March 11 to make 400 million barrels available from emergency reserves. As shown below, this is not the first time it has happened.

However, this 400M barrel is the largest stock release in the agency’s history.

The DOE reported that the U.S. share will be 172 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, beginning next week, with deliveries taking about 120 days at planned discharge rates. As seen below, the U.S. SRP stocks have declined from the 2021 levels, and at an average U.S. oil consumption of 20 million barrels/day, 172M barrels account for 9 days of consumption.

Bottom line: I don't expect oil prices to return to pre-Iran war levels due to this emergency stock release unless tankers resume their journeys through the Strait of Hormuz.

Given the escalations in the conflict that I discussed in the last section, I don't think this event is anywhere near.

Therefore, even after the February CPI print matched the Street's consensus (headline CPI at +0.3% m/m and +2.4% y/y; core CPI was +0.2% m/m and +2.5% y/y), some analysts are pushing back the date for the next Fed easing call. A notable example is Goldman Sachs, which pushed its Fed easing call back to September and December from June and September.

Since the start of the war, Polymarket is showing a spike in the probability of no change in interest rates for the June 17 meeting (the first with Warsh as the Fed Chair).

Here is where I think markets get it wrong.

It is my view that, against all odds, Warsh will cut in his first FOMC meeting. In fact, I think the current consensus of cuts for 2026 is way too conservative. As seen below, the market is only pricing in a rate cut by the December 9, 2026, meeting.

One! (notice the exclamation mark).

Given that Trump nominated Warsh and considering Trump’s (unrealistic, in my view) goal of pushing interest rates down to 1%, I believe Warsh will face significant pressure to cut rates, even if the macro data suggests otherwise. In other words, I strongly believe the Fed may allow the economy to run hot in the back half of the year.

Overall, despite the risk to the Fed’s independence, I think the markets would more than welcome a rate cut. And that's why I'm bullish for the second half of the year. The first half is very volatile, and I see two negative catalysts ahead.

I'm talking about the March PPI and CPI reports. Given the 20% increase in oil prices, I think both are going to come in hot.

That said, I expect a jump in the SPY if a ceasefire is agreed upon. That's why I took profits and closed my earlier shorts (in the form of put options) in the last days.

In the midst of the volatility, I reiterate my hold rating on the broader markets. In my view, a correction in the SPY to the 610-620 level is likely if the markets get nervous heading into the June meeting. A contributing factor could be Japan raising its interest rate, followed by (less likely, in my view) the ECB.

data.title: Shift4 Vs. Global Payments: Which Is The Better Recovery Play?
data.description:
Intro

A month ago, when I analyzed the beaten‑down payment‑sector giants PayPal (PYPL) and Fiserv (FISV), I realized they weren’t the only fintech companies facing significant pressure. Reviewing the one‑year chart and comparing the biggest names in the payments space, I wrote, "PayPal, down 47%, ranks third from the bottom, almost on par with Shift4Payments (FOUR), which is down 51%."

In addition to FOUR’s notably weak performance, Global Payments Inc. (GPN) also stood out within the peer group as the clear valuation outlier, trading at the lowest P/E multiples:

Given their weak stock performances but low valuation multiples, I decided to take a closer look at FOUR and GPN at the first opportunity to determine whether there are serious company‑specific reasons behind their sharp selloffs or whether the reaction might simply reflect a broader sector‑wide weakness, especially since leaders PayPal and Fiserv are also under significant pressure.

The Similarities Between the Two

GPN and FOUR are not truly comparable in scale. Global Payments is one of the largest payment processors in the world, handling about $3.7 trillion in gross payment volume following the Worldpay acquisition. FOUR, by contrast, is a much smaller, niche player focused on hospitality and experiences, processing just over $200 billion in volume. While historically centered in the U.S., it has recently expanded overseas, bolstering its global presence through last year’s acquisition of tax-free payments leader Global Blue.

What the two companies do share—beyond similarly low valuation multiples—is their position in the same part of the payments value chain: both operate in the Acquiring segment, offering merchant solutions. The key difference lies in their strategic focus. Global Payments, as a leading global provider, offers broad, universal solutions across virtually all sectors, whereas FOUR concentrates on the so‑called “experience economy,” serving restaurants, hotels, casinos, entertainment venues, and similar verticals.

The next slide illustrates their positioning well, with both companies operating in Acquiring:

Let’s take a closer look at the details of the companies.

Brief Overview

Shift4

Shift4 was founded in a New Jersey basement in 1999 by a then‑16‑year‑old Jared Isaacman, a highly successful entrepreneur who is also a professional pilot and astronaut. Led by its exceptional founder, the company has grown into a major payments player across the hospitality and entertainment sectors, holding leading positions in several categories. Shift4’s strength comes from offering a fully integrated stack of hardware, software, and payments. While historically focused on the U.S., Shift4 has also recently expanded internationally.

The latest quarterly results included a slide that presented the company’s exceptional growth story very well, showing a 4.5‑fold expansion since 2021:

The company outlines the unique qualities and value proposition of its business:

I think the company did a great job in their latest shareholder letter, offering a well-crafted explanation of their unique spot in the marketplace:

In a world where AI is evolving rapidly and investors are struggling to pick the future winners, I will remind investors of this: We offer a physical payment experience in environments that demand a face-to-face interaction. We also focus predominantly on merchants that need a lot of software to run their business.

As previously noted, FOUR has recently expanded aggressively internationally, fueled by acquisitions. The latest and largest of these was Global Blue, completed in July last year.

Q4 FY2025 Earnings and 2026 Outlook

While the reported earnings were largely in line, the outlook for 2026 disappointed investors. The EPS outlook for next year came in significantly below Wall Street’s consensus estimate of $6.45. In addition, the company’s Q1 revenue guidance of $548 million also fell short of Street expectations of $579 million.

The outlook presented for 2026:

The weak outlook sparked a sharp selloff, with the stock dropping about 15% from around $57 to $48 the day after the announcement. The bottom around $43, formed a few days after earnings, is currently being tested again as geopolitical tensions have fueled a broader market drawdown.

Global Payments

The company presents itself:

Global Payments (NYSE: GPN) is a leading payment technology and software company that powers commerce for businesses of all sizes worldwide. We help businesses grow with confidence by delivering innovative solutions that enable seamless payment acceptance, smarter operations and exceptional client experiences – online, in store and everywhere in between.

With its global reach, local expertise and scale, Global Payments manages trillions in payments volume and billions of transactions across more than 175 countries. Headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia, Global Payments is a Fortune 500® company and a member of the S&P 500.

Global Payments traces its roots back to National Data Corporation, an American IT and payments company founded in 1967 by retired U.S. Air Force Colonel George Thorpe. NDC grew into the world’s largest payments processor by volume in the late 1980s and later spun off its eCommerce division, Global Payment Systems, which became Global Payments in 2001. Since the spinoff, Global Payments has operated as an independent, publicly traded company on the NYSE under the ticker “GPN.”

Following the acquisition of WorldPay last year and the sale of the issuer solutions division, the combined company now presents itself:

Q4 FY2025 Earnings and Outlook

In contrast to Shift4’s earnings release and the subsequent 15% selloff, Global Payments jumped 16% after the company issued a strong outlook for 2026 and announced that its board had authorized up to $2.5B in share buybacks, including a $550M accelerated repurchase program. The company’s 2026 adjusted EPS guidance of $13.80–$14.00 came in above the Street’s estimate of $13.78. Constant‑currency adjusted revenue is expected to grow around 5%, compared with 1.8% growth in 2025.

Comparison

FOUR GPN
The core problem Has recently been falling short of earnings expectations frequently. Seen as a slower mover due to its scale and complexity compared to newer competitors like Stripe, Block, Adyen, and others.
2026 revenue growth 26-31% 5%
2027-2028 revenue growth ~15% ~6%
2026 EPS growth ~8% ~13%
2027-2028 EPS growth ~21% ~17%
EV/EBITDA 2026 7.6 6
EV/Free Cash Flow 2026 14.6 12.2
Net Debt/Ebitda fwd ~3 ~3
Projected share repurchase 2026 ~500 million ~2 billion

Valuation

As shown in the previous comparison table, FOUR has a forward-looking EV/EBITDA of 7.6 and an EV/FCF of 14.4, while GPN’s figures are 6 and 12.2, respectively. While FOUR expects solid 2026 revenue growth of over 20%, its free cash flow is projected to remain at around $500 million, the same as in 2025. From 2027, however, EPS growth is expected to pick up to about 21%, while GPN is projected to see 17% bottom-line growth.

These growth rates and multiples, in my view, suggest both companies are slightly undervalued. I still favor GPN, as the scale gained from the Worldpay acquisition gives it an edge, as I see it, and being a leader in the sector is an advantage. The pure-play structure after the issuance processing sale is also highly positive.

FOUR shines on accelerating global expansion and double-digit revenue growth expectations. While free cash flow and profitability are under pressure going into 2026, it could get better once the Global Blue integration costs subside.

I rate both companies as Buy. While they may appear significantly undervalued at first glance when looking at plain P/E multiples, the reality is more nuanced: both carry substantial debt loads following recent M&A activity, which makes straightforward P/E ratios look misleadingly low. That said, in my view, trading at an EV/EBITDA of around 6–7 — a metric that better reflects higher leverage — is still attractive for companies with potential double‑digit earnings growth operating in a secular growth industry.

Risks

In my assessment, the primary risk facing FOUR is its relatively small scale. While I see the payments segment where FOUR and GPN operate as less risky than other parts of the value chain, which may face major structural changes in the coming years, the competitive landscape for FOUR is still very intense. Smaller players like FOUR may be at a disadvantage if larger competitors adopt more aggressive strategies in the market subsegments where FOUR currently leads.

For GPN, I think the most significant risk near-term stems from challenges related to the Worldpay acquisition. The integration of two large organizations inherently involves increased execution risks. GPN might also face hurdles from slow innovation and a lag in adapting to marketplace shifts, as seen with companies like PayPal and Fiserv. Nevertheless, GPN’s pure-play focus within a segment currently seen as resilient offers some mitigation against these risks in my view.

Takeaway

After taking a deeper look, neither FOUR nor GPN is as undervalued as they first appeared based on simple P/E multiples. Both companies have taken on meaningful debt through recent M&A, but in my view, their valuations still remain attractive.

FOUR’s acquisition of Global Blue strengthens its international reach and adds diversification, while GPN’s purchase of Worldpay reinforces its position as a global leader in merchant acquiring.

Importantly, the segment they operate in is, in my view, less risky than the issuing side of the payments value chain, which is currently on the verge of major structural shifts driven by the rapid adoption of Apple Pay, the growing popularity of digital wallets, and emerging stablecoin initiatives.

Overall, if I had to choose a winner, I would lean toward GPN due to its scale and fundamentals. Still, I think both deserve Buy ratings, though given today’s geopolitical uncertainty, these come with a degree of caution.

data.title: PBF Energy: Iran Tailwind Factored In
data.description:
Shares of PBF Energy Inc. (PBF) have been a strong performer over the past year, more than doubling in value. The company has benefited from a much better refining environment after a weak period in the winter of 2024-2025, and it is making solid progress recovering from a fire at its Martinez refinery. More recently, the Iran war has created severe dislocations in the physical petroleum markets, potentially providing enhanced opportunities for refineries.

After having been a long-term bull, I downgraded PBF to a “Hold” in October, and this was overly cautious as the stock has gained a further 50% since then. With updated financials and a much different macro environment, now is a good time to revisit PBF.

Iran Conflict Is A Significant Tailwind

When looking at an oil-related stock, there must first be a conversation about the Iran conflict. I will be the first to admit that when I last covered PBF, a war in Iran was far from my mind and an upside risk I gave no consideration to. I am sure I am not alone in that regard. Before this conflict began, shares were closer to $35, so PBF has rallied 20% as a result of oil market movements since then. The reason for this is quite clear.

The war is significantly disrupting the flows of crude oil and refined products through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the key passages of the global oil trade. We are likely seeing a 10%-20% outage of supply, and this scramble for physical supply is pushing prices up across the petroleum complex. Refiners make money on the spread between crude oil and products (like gasoline), known as the crack spread, so when crack spreads are, for example, $25, a refinery should generate a $25 gross margin from processing one barrel of crude oil and turning it into gasoline and diesel. As you can see below, spreads have surged past $40. As a reminder, I view ~$25 as the long-term norm for spreads, so refineries are making an extra $15-$20 for every barrel they process.

This is obviously an excellent situation to be in. As long as the war continues and flows through the Strait are impaired, I expect crack spreads to remain extremely wide. Countries like China are also banning refined product exports to ensure adequate domestic supply, which serves to further tighten the market and enhance margins. So long as refineries can source crude oil to run at capacity (which should be no problem for U.S. refiners), this is an ideal moneymaking environment.

Of course, this environment will not last forever, and this outsized profit is, in my view, more one-time in nature. Still, it comes at an excellent time for PBF, which is focused on restoring financial strength after a challenging 2025. I also now expect crack spreads to remain elevated for some time, and there will likely be higher spreads even after hostilities end and the Strait is fully operational. We do not even know when that is yet, and my base case is for crack spreads to stay north of $30 at least through June.

I caveat that view with one risk. Because of our refining capacity, the United States is a significant exporter of refined fuels, exporting about 300 million barrels of gasoline last year, for instance. That is nearly 1mb/d. As noted above about China, we have already begun to see a rise in energy protectionism. The longer this crisis goes on, the greater the potential for the United States to impose similar restrictions. This is not my base case and would be very controversial with our allies. However, such a ban would trap more gasoline in the United States, lower prices, and impair refiners’ profitability. If this policy comes into play, I would expect refining stocks to give back a significant portion of their wartime gains.

Insurance Proceeds Add To A Solid Q4

It is also important to note that PBF Energy’s financial performance was improving even before the war as the refining environment improved. In Q4, it generated a surprise profit of $0.66, blowing past estimates by $0.77. Adjusted EBITDA was $258 million, a substantial swing from the $250 million it lost in Q4 2024.

Throughput was a solid 889,000 bpd in Q4, and its refining margins were up over $6 to $11.16. Operating costs were flat at $8. Now, I do expect operating costs to increase in 2026 as refineries face some pressure from higher electricity prices, and I see about $0.50 of upside risk. Still, this is overwhelmed by the wider crack spread.

The majority of PBF’s crude input is heavy and sour crudes. With improved relations with Venezuela, the U.S. supply of such crude oil will likely be higher going forward, likely enhancing the margins of processing this type of oil. Each $1 improvement in the crude differential has a $150-$200 million benefit in my estimation. Management estimates that it achieved $230 million in run-rate savings during 2025 with a target of achieving another $120 million of run-rate costs in 2026. I remain cautious about the ability to sustain these cost cuts. Indeed, given scheduled turnaround activity, cap-ex needs will likely be higher in 2026.

During Q4, it received $394 million of insurance recoveries, bringing the full-year total to $894 million. I expect the company to receive at least another $50 million in proceeds from its insurers, and frankly, the company has done an excellent job quickly receiving proceeds, which has helped to largely insulate PBF from what would otherwise have been a very severe financial impact. PBF’s Martinez refinery should resume full operations during March. Given other refinery closures in California, this facility should see improved profitability with less competition in the heavily insulated California gasoline market.

Iran Windfall Will Accelerate Balance Sheet Repair

Turning to its balance sheet, PBF ended the year with $530 million of cash and $1.6 billion of net debt. Net debt to capital is 28%, up from 16% a year ago. The refining business is capital intensive and highly cyclical, so I like to see capital structures have limited debt, ideally under 20% debt to capital. As such, I see about $500 million of debt reduction needs over time. Shares offer a 2.6% dividend yield, which I view as secure, but I view buybacks as unlikely until debt is reduced. The windfall from higher crack spreads today should accelerate the pace of debt reduction.

Essentially, every day that crack spreads are north of $40, PBF is generating an ~$11 million windfall, or $0.10/day of earnings power. As such, I think we are conservatively looking at $600 million of free cash flow this year, up from my $300 million long-term view. If this crisis continues for longer, my view could be too cautious. With the company likely to generate ~$6 of free cash flow this year, shares do not appear expensive at just 7x. That said, at least $2.50 of this is unlikely to repeat, unless you expect the Strait to remain unnavigable into 2027. In such an environment, we would also likely see a demand-destroying recession that pushes spreads down eventually.

At a ~7% long-run free cash flow yield, valuation is getting fairly full, and I continue to prefer names like Marathon (MPC) or Delek (DK). Given the direction the conflict appears to be going, I do not see a need to sell PBF here, but it increasingly feels too late to buy. If there is a quicker resolution of the conflict, I would expect shares to pull back below $40. Since that is not my base case and I am concerned about longer disruptions, I am maintaining a “Hold," even after this large run, with this windfall helping to accelerate its debt reduction needs.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: Defensive Hedge Against Market Volatility
data.description:
As rising market uncertainties take hold of U.S. equities, I believe that a defensive posture is looking more and more attractive as each trading day closes. Investors could perhaps find this defensiveness in the toothpaste aisle of Procter & Gamble (PG).

The maker of Crest toothpaste and Pantene Shampoo, among other staples, pays a quarterly dividend of $1.06/share, and shares have generally traded in a stable range through its storied history.

Of late, however, the stock has been nursing losses, along with the broader markets, and one could be forgiven for questioning whether the losses are justified. Though the stock is up 5% YTD, shares are down about 7% over the past month.

At the midpoint of its 52-week range and with a defensive hedge as one catalyst, I believe the stock is worth additional consideration.

PG Stock Key Metrics

PG currently commands a forward trading multiple of about 22x earnings. This is roughly in line with the broader market indexes and its own historical averages. I don’t find the valuation particularly compelling at these levels.

Neither do the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants, which see PG as a "Hold," due primarily to its higher trading multiple.

The broader SA analyst community is likewise neutral, with most coverage over the past year to that effect.

Those on Wall Street, on the other hand, see more value at current levels and are bullish on PG’s prospects. Average price targets from the group peg the stock's value at just shy of $170/share.

This would indicate over 10% upside potential from current levels. While that’s certainly a positive, I don’t necessarily know if that is inspiring enough from an investment standpoint.

PG Operating Performance

Though PG is a staple company, that doesn’t exactly mean consumers are rushing to their product lines. In Q2, for example, PG reported its softest quarter of its fiscal year, with results reflecting a mix of temporary headwinds and uneven global demand trends.

At the topline, organic sales were essentially flat YOY, with pricing contributing one point of growth while volumes also declined by roughly one point. CFO Andre Schulten attributed this to unusual base-period dynamics in the prior year. Or, more specifically, consumer pantry loading tied to port strikes and hurricane disruptions in the United States.

Despite the unfavorable comparison, the underlying business still showed pockets of strength, in my view. Seven of P&G’s 10 product categories, for example, held or grew organic sales during the quarter.

Hair Care led the way with mid-single-digit growth, while Skin and Personal Care, Personal Health Care, Home Care, and Oral Care each posted modest gains. Some of the pressure came from Family Care, which declined sharply due primarily to the earlier inventory build.

Geographically, the story was also somewhat more mixed. North America sales slipped modestly, but many international markets continued to show healthier momentum.

Latin America delivered particularly strong growth, while Greater China posted a solid increase despite what was described as a still-challenging consumer environment.

More to the bottom line, core EPS landed at $1.88/share, roughly in line with the prior year. Margins, however, contracted as the company continued investing in innovation, marketing, and productivity initiatives aimed at strengthening growth in the back half of the fiscal year.

What is the Outlook For PG Stock?

Looking ahead, PG is projecting that the softer results seen in the latest quarter will give way to stronger performance in the back half of the fiscal year.

In providing guidance, PG reiterated its full-year guidance. To me, this indicates that recent investments in innovation, brand building, and retail execution are expected to begin showing up more meaningfully in upcoming quarters.

Within the guidance, PG continues to expect organic sales growth ranging from flat to roughly 4%. That outlook is broadly consistent with the company’s long-term growth algorithm, though management acknowledged that growth will likely land toward the lower end of that range as the year progresses.

Importantly, the guidance assumes global market growth of about 2% across the categories in which the company competes. I believe this is reasonable, even with the headwinds currently afflicting the global economies.

At the bottom line, PG expects core EPS at a midpoint of $6.96/share for the fiscal year, representing growth of up to 4% compared to the prior period.

Is PG Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

I ultimately view shares in PG as a "Hold" at current levels.

I do believe PG’s defensive staples-focused business model can be particularly attractive during periods of heightened market volatility, such as the one we appear to be in now. History has shown that when investors grow uneasy about the economic outlook, they often gravitate toward companies that sell everyday necessities, and PG fits that mold quite well, in my view.

That said, I don’t necessarily find the valuation compelling enough to justify adding shares right now. Trading at roughly 22x forward earnings, the stock appears fairly valued relative to both its historical averages and the broader market.

While Wall Street price targets imply roughly 10% upside, I question whether that level of potential return is particularly enticing given the limited growth profile typical of mature consumer staples businesses.

Still, I believe the company’s steady cash generation and shareholder-friendly capital return policies help support a "Hold" to "Bullish" view. PG continues to produce strong free cash flow and plans to return roughly +$15 billion to shareholders this year through dividends and share repurchases. That type of consistency can provide stability in uncertain markets, in my view.

For investors already holding the stock, I believe PG remains a solid portfolio hedge, and I continue to view shares as a "Hold." For new investors, I would be more compelled with a better entry point, about 5% to 10% below current trading levels.

data.title: Shipping Stocks Catch A Windfall As Freight Markets Go Vertical
data.description:
Markets hate uncertainty, and right now there’s plenty to go around. The outbreak of the U.S.-Iran conflict, followed by Iranian retaliation against oil infrastructure across the Persian Gulf, has sent crude prices surging and shipping rates soaring to record levels.

I get the urge to reach for the sell button. When you see crude oil jumping to seven-month highs in a matter of days, it’s natural to want to head for the exit. But if decades of investing through geopolitical crises have taught me anything, it’s this: don’t panic.

The data, history, and consensus view from the world’s leading investment banks all point in the same direction. This conflict is likely to be intense but short-lived, and the investors who keep their composure will be the ones best positioned when the dust settles.

The Facts on the Ground

The U.S. and Israel launched joint airstrikes across Iran on February 28, and Iran’s supreme leader was confirmed killed. Iran retaliated by striking oil and gas facilities in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, and Kuwait, taking capacity offline and effectively shutting down transit through the all-important Strait of Hormuz.

Indeed, Hormuz is the jugular of global energy. Roughly a quarter of the world’s oil consumption passes through it, and vessels are now having to avoid the waterway due to the risk of attack and insurers cancelling war risk cover.

The disruption is already showing up across the supply chain.

For instance, the cost of hiring a supertanker to ship 2 million barrels of crude from the U.S. Gulf Coast to China has reportedly hit an unbelievable $29 million, a new record, according to the Baltic Exchange in London. Shipping oil from the Arab Gulf to India has exploded from between $50,000 and $100,000 to an eye-watering $477,000 per day. Meanwhile, Maersk and CMA CHM have rolled out emergency surcharges and, in CMA’s case, suspended bookings for hazardous goods across a huge portion of the region.

Why I Believe This Will Be Short-Lived

Despite the dramatic headlines, I believe the evidence strongly favors a brief conflict.

For one, Iran is outmatched. According to CLSA, U.S. military spending exceeds Iran’s by a factor of 126 to 1. Without boots on the ground, this is more likely to resemble the limited strikes in 2024 and mid-2025 than a prolonged occupation like Iraq.

Two, there’s a powerful political incentive to wrap this up quickly. The Trump administration is acutely aware that energy prices are a kitchen-table issue heading into the 2026 midterms. A Morgan Stanley analysis noted that public approval for the strikes sits at only about 27%. A short, decisive operation lets the administration claim a national security win without saddling voters with $4-plus gasoline.

And three, the Strait of Hormuz is simply too important to stay closed for long. It’s a vital artery for seaborne oil with few alternative routes. Fitch Ratings expects the effective closure to be temporary, and the U.S. is already mulling Navy escorts similar to the tanker wars of the 1980s. The global economy can’t function without this checkpoint, and every major power knows it.

What History Tells Us About Oil Shocks

This isn’t the first time geopolitical events have sent oil prices spiking, and the pattern is remarkably consistent. Oil prices had already been climbing on geopolitical risk premium before the first strikes, and that premium tends to fade once the immediate threat subsides. Iran and Israel exchanged missile strikes twice in 2024 and again in mid-2025. Each time, oil continued to flow and prices retraced.

Goldman Sachs' range of scenarios is instructive. They estimate oil price increases of between $1 to $15 per barrel depending on the extent and duration of Strait closures, with offsets like spare pipeline capacity and strategic petroleum reserves providing cushions. Even Bloomberg’s more alarming $108-a-barrel scenario assumes a prolonged closure, which, as I’ve already outlined, is the low-probability tail risk.

Where I See the Opportunities

I see the current developments as presenting both near-term challenges and significant opportunities.

Shipping is the immediate beneficiary. The near-halt of transport in the Strait of Hormuz has repriced global freight markets overnight. This is a windfall for shipping companies, and the elevated rates are already locked in regardless of whether the conflict ends tomorrow or in a month.

Energy and commodities look well supported. Even before the conflict, UBS saw further upside for broad commodities in 2026, driven by metals. The geopolitical premium on oil adds another layer. UBS specifically recommends actively managed commodity strategies given the elevated intra-commodity volatility. That makes for an environment that I believe favors active, nimble approaches over passive indexing.

Airlines face a short-term headwind but a medium-term opportunity. Yes, jet fuel costs are rising, and that puts pressure on margins. But many airlines, particularly European and certain Latin American and Asia-Pacific carriers, maintain hedging programs. More importantly, history shows that consumer travel demand rebounds after initial disruptions. If the conflict is as short as the consensus expects, airlines represent a classic recovery trade, similar to what we saw during the pandemic.

The Bottom Line

I’ve been in the business long enough to have lived through the Gulf War, 9/11, Iraq, the Arab Spring and countless other geopolitical shocks. Every single time, the temptation to sell everything and hide in cash was enormous, and every single time, the investors who stayed the course came out ahead.

JPMorgan put it well in their latest note. They write that, “through countless crises, wars, pandemics, and recessions, investors who have stayed the course have recouped losses and benefited from growth, innovation, and progress.” That doesn’t mean you should be reckless. As always, I recommend a 10% weighting in gold, which can enhance diversification and serve as a buffer against geopolitical risk.

As CLSA wisely cautioned, wars and financial shocks hit your highest-conviction trades hardest, because that’s where people take profits in a panic. The problem is that if you cut your strongest positions now, you risk being badly positioned for the snapback.

The fundamentals of the global economy have not changed. Travel demand is intact. Shipping companies are earning record rates. And energy markets are functioning, even if they’re under stress. The most likely outcome is that this conflict resolves in days or weeks, and oil eases back. The investors who held their nerves might be glad they did.
